title: What to know before using Nerium oleander (Oleander)?
intro_desc: Nerium oleander, a toxic evergreen shrub native to areas surrounding the Mediterranean Sea, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes despite its poisonous nature. Its medicinal properties include the ability to treat heart conditions, reduce swelling, and alleviate pain, although it must be used with extreme caution due to the risk of fatal poisoning. Nerium oleander thrives in full sun and well-drained soil, making it a popular ornamental plant in many regions. The plant's botanical characteristics include clusters of showy, five-lobed, trumpet-shaped flowers that bloom in shades of pink, red, white, and yellow, and narrow, lance-shaped leaves that grow in pairs along the stem.
medicine_intro: Nerium oleander is used to treat a variety of health conditions, including cardiac arrhythmia, congestive heart failure, and high blood pressure. The active constituents of Nerium oleander include oleandrin, oleandroside, and nerioside, which have been found to have cardiotonic, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory properties. Medicinal preparations of Nerium oleander include extracts, teas, and tinctures made from the leaves, flowers, and roots of the plant. However, it is important to note that Nerium oleander is a highly toxic plant and can cause serious side effects, including nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and seizures, even at low doses. Precautions must be taken when handling the plant, and it is recommended that only experienced healthcare professionals administer medicinal preparations made from Nerium oleander. Additionally, it is not recommended for self-medication, as the therapeutic and toxic doses are very close, and it can be fatal if not used properly.
medicine_desc: Nerium oleander has several medicinal uses, such as treating heart conditions, reducing inflammation, and alleviating respiratory problems. Its therapeutic potential can be attributed to the presence of various bioactive compounds, including cardiac glycosides, flavonoids, and alkaloids.

The medicinal constituents of Nerium oleander have been extensively studied for their cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant properties. Cardiac glycosides, such as oleandrin and oleandrigenin, are the most notable compounds in oleander. These compounds have a positive inotropic effect on the heart, increasing its contractility and improving its efficiency. Flavonoids and alkaloids also contribute to oleander's medicinal properties, exhibiting anti-inflammatory and expectorant effects.

The medicinal preparations of Nerium oleander typically involve extracting the bioactive compounds from its leaves, stems, or roots. The extracts can then be formulated into various products, such as tinctures, capsules, and ointments. The most common preparation is a water-based extract, which is used to treat heart conditions, inflammation, and respiratory ailments. However, it is essential to note that oleander is highly toxic, and any medicinal preparations must be formulated with extreme care and precision.

Despite its medicinal benefits, Nerium oleander also has several side effects that must be carefully considered before using it as a treatment. The most common side effects include gastrointestinal disturbances, such as nausea and vomiting, and cardiac arrhythmias. Overdosing on oleander can result in more severe side effects, including seizures, paralysis, and even death. Therefore, it is crucial to consult with a healthcare professional before using oleander for medicinal purposes.

Precautions must be taken when using Nerium oleander for medicinal purposes. It is essential to use only small doses, and any medicinal preparations must be formulated by a trained professional. Pregnant women and children should avoid using oleander, as it can have harmful effects on fetal development and growth. It is also essential to keep oleander away from pets, as it can be fatal to animals. Finally, it is crucial to store oleander extracts and preparations out of reach of children and pets.
horticulture_intro: Nerium oleander needs full sun to thrive, meaning at least six hours of direct sunlight each day. It prefers well-drained soil and can tolerate drought, but regular watering will promote blooming. When planting, ensure there is enough space for the plant to grow, as it can reach up to 20 feet tall and 10 feet wide. Regular pruning will help keep its size under control and encourage bushier growth. Oleander blooms throughout the summer and into the fall, and spent flowers should be removed to promote new growth. Although oleander is relatively low-maintenance, it is susceptible to pests such as scales, mealybugs, and whiteflies, as well as diseases like root rot and powdery mildew. Regularly inspecting the plant and using appropriate treatments when necessary can help keep it healthy.
horticulture_desc: Nerium oleander has specific growth requirements. It thrives in USDA hardiness zones 8-11, where the temperature ranges between 15-40 degrees Fahrenheit. Oleander prefers full sun exposure and can tolerate partial shade, but flowering may be reduced. It requires well-drained soil, as it is susceptible to root rot in wet or soggy conditions. Oleander can tolerate a wide range of soil types, including sandy, loamy, and clay soils, as long as they are well-drained. It is drought-tolerant and can survive in nutrient-poor soils.

When it comes to planting, spring or fall is the best time to plant oleander. Choose a location with adequate space, as it can grow up to 12-20 feet tall and 10-15 feet wide. Before planting, amend the soil with compost or well-rotted manure to improve soil structure and moisture retention. When planting, dig a hole twice as wide and deep as the root ball. Place the plant in the hole and backfill with soil, tamping it down firmly. Water thoroughly and mulch around the base to retain moisture and suppress weeds.

Caring for oleander requires minimal effort. It is a low-maintenance plant that requires little watering once established. However, it benefits from occasional watering during prolonged dry spells. Fertilize oleander once a year in the spring with a balanced, slow-release fertilizer. Prune oleander to maintain shape, control size, and promote bushiness. Pruning can be done at any time of the year, but it is best to avoid pruning during the hot summer months. Deadhead spent flowers to encourage new blooms. Oleander is tolerant of heavy pruning and can be cut back to the ground if necessary.

Harvesting oleander is not a common practice, as its toxic sap can cause skin irritation and other health issues. All parts of the plant are toxic, including the leaves, stems, and flowers. However, if one wants to harvest oleander, it is essential to take precautions. Wear protective clothing, gloves, and eyewear when handling the plant. It is best to harvest oleander in the morning when the sap content is lowest. After harvesting, wash hands and tools thoroughly. Dry the leaves and stems in a well-ventilated area away from direct sunlight. Once dry, store them in an airtight container in a cool, dry place.

Oleander is susceptible to several pests and diseases. Common pests include aphids, scale insects, mealybugs, and whiteflies. These pests can be controlled with insecticidal soap or horticultural oil. Neem oil can also be used as a preventative measure. Oleander is prone to fungal diseases such as root rot, powdery mildew, and leaf spot. To prevent fungal diseases, avoid overwatering and provide good air circulation around the plant. Fungicides can be used to treat existing infections. Oleander is also susceptible to nematodes, which can be controlled with nematicides. Regularly inspect oleander for signs of pests and diseases and take action promptly.
botany_intro: Nerium oleander belongs to the Apocynaceae family and is the sole species in the Nerium genus. It is a evergreen shrub that can grow up to 20 feet tall, with long narrow leaves that are 2-8 inches long and 0.1-0.5 inches wide. The flowers of Nerium oleander are seen in shades of pink, red, yellow, and white, and are known for their pleasant fragrance. There are several variants of Nerium oleander, including 'Hawaii', 'Hardy Pink', and 'Petite Pink', which differ in their flower color, size, and cold tolerance. This plant is native to regions around the Mediterranean Sea and thrives in USDA hardiness zones 8-11. It grows naturally in sandy and rocky soils, as well as in disturbed areas. Nerium oleander is a perennial plant, which means it lives for more than two years, and reproduces through both seeds and suckers. It has a rapid growth rate and is known for its drought tolerance.
botany_desc: Nerium oleander is a toxic evergreen shrub that belongs to the dogbane family, Apocynaceae, and the genus Nerium. This family includes approximately 400 species of plants, mainly distributed in the tropical and subtropical regions of the world. The oleander is one of the most popular and widely cultivated ornamental plants in this family, valued for its attractive and long-lasting flowers, as well as its hardiness and adaptability to different climates.

Morphologically, Nerium oleander is a woody plant that can grow up to 20 feet tall and 15 feet wide, with a loose, spreading, and irregular shape. Its leaves are simple, lanceolate, and arranged in opposite pairs along the stem. They are thick, leathery, and dark green, with a prominent midrib and entire margins. The flowers are showy, fragrant, and arranged in terminal or axillary cymes. They have five petals, fused at the base, and varying in color from white, pink, red, yellow, or purple, depending on the cultivar. The fruit is a long, slender, paired follicle, containing numerous seeds with tufts of white hairs that facilitate their dispersal by the wind.

Nerium oleander has several variant names and cultivars, differing in their flower color, size, shape, and habit. Some of the most popular ones are 'Algiers', 'Aurora', 'Boticelli', 'Carnea', 'Compacta', 'DWARF PINK', 'Hawaii', 'Luteum', 'Petite Pink', 'Rosea', 'Sofia', 'Ventricosa', and 'Yellow'. These cultivars have been selected for their ornamental value and resistance to pests, diseases, or adverse environmental conditions. However, they all share the same toxicity and require the same cultural practices for their optimal growth and development.

Geographically, Nerium oleander is native to the Mediterranean region, from Portugal and Morocco in the west, to Iran and the Arabian Peninsula in the east. It is also naturalized in many other parts of the world, including Australia, South Africa, California, and Florida, where it has escaped from cultivation and become an invasive species. It prefers sunny, dry, and well-drained locations, such as roadside banks, coastal dunes, and disturbed areas. It can tolerate drought, salinity, wind, and mild frost, making it a versatile and low-maintenance plant.

Finally, Nerium oleander has a perennial life-cycle, with a rapid growth rate and a long lifespan. It can propagate by seeds, cuttings, or layering. The seeds need warm temperatures and light to germinate, and they can take several weeks or months to emerge. The cuttings can be taken from the current season's growth, and they should be treated with rooting hormone and placed in a sandy or peaty medium. The layering can be done by bending a low-hanging branch to the ground, covering it with soil, and securing it with a pin or a stone. Once the new plant has established its own root system, it can be separated from the mother plant and transplanted to its permanent location. The oleander benefits from regular pruning, fertilization, and watering, but it is also resistant to many pests and diseases, such as aphids, scales, mealybugs, whiteflies, powdery mildew, and root rot. However, it is susceptible to some fungal and bacterial diseases, such as Botryosphaeria branch canker, Verticillium wilt, and Fusarium wilt, which can cause dieback or even death of the plant.
history_intro: Nerium oleander has been used historically for as a medicine for various ailments, including heart conditions, epilepsy, and leprosy, despite its high toxicity. In Greek mythology, oleander was associated with death and was said to have flowered on the grave of Leander, a young man who drowned while swimming across the Hellespont to see his lover Hero. Ancient rituals involving oleander include its use in crowns worn during festivals, although this was later abandoned due to the plant's toxicity. The poisonous properties of oleander have been documented in literature, such as in the works of Shakespeare and Wilde, often symbolizing betrayal, deceit, and death. The plant's symbolism extends to its use as a funerary flower in some cultures, representing the fleeting nature of life.
history_desc: Nerium oleander has several historical medicinal uses, such as treating ailments related to the heart, skin, and even leprosy. Its roots, leaves, and flowers have been used in various forms, such as decoctions, powders, and ointments. However, it is important to note that all parts of the oleander plant contain toxic compounds, and its use as a medicine should only be done under the supervision of a trained medical professional.

In Greek mythology, oleander is associated with the story of Daphne, a nymph pursued by Apollo. According to the myth, Daphne pleaded to the river god Peneus to save her from Apollo's advances, and Peneus transformed her into a laurel tree. However, some versions of the myth claim that Daphne was transformed into an oleander tree instead, and its toxic properties reflect the bitterness and sadness of Daphne's fate.

The use of oleander in ancient rituals can be traced back to the ancient Egyptians, who used it in embalming practices. The ancient Greeks and Romans also used oleander in their rituals, often associating it with death and funerals. The toxic properties of the plant made it a symbol of danger, and it was often used in rituals to ward off evil spirits. In addition, oleander was also associated with the underworld and was believed to have the power to communicate with the dead.

Literature has long been fascinated with the oleander's toxicity and symbolism. In ancient Roman literature, the poet Horace wrote about the oleander's fatal effects, while the poet Virgil used it as a symbol of deceit and treachery. In more recent literature, the oleander has been featured in novels such as "The Secret Garden" by Frances Hodgson Burnett and "The Age of Innocence" by Edith Wharton. In both novels, the oleander serves as a metaphor for the hidden dangers and secrets that lurk beneath the surface of society.

In conclusion, the historical aspects of Nerium oleander are varied and complex, reflecting its toxicity, symbolism, and cultural significance. From its medicinal uses in ancient times to its association with mythology, literature, and rituals, the oleander has played a prominent role in human history. However, it is important to remember that the oleander's toxicity can have fatal consequences, and its use should be approached with caution and respect.
